tasks.title: Fix `macos-ci` target ignoring failures from build and test steps
tasks.description: The `macos-ci` target in the `Makefile` improperly chains sub-targets using `; \` within a single shell block without `set -e`. This allows subsequent commands (including the final success echo) to execute even if `macos-build` or `macos-test` fails with a non-zero exit code. In the reported logs, `xcodebuild` failed with `Error 65`, yet the CI gate reported `✓ macOS CI completed` and the task was marked as done. This target must be refactored to either use proper `make` dependencies or ensure that any failure immediately halts execution and returns an error.
evidence: User logs show 'make[1]: *** [macos-test] Error 65' followed by '✓ macOS CI completed'
Makefile lines 196-200 chain commands with ';' in a single shell block without 'set -e'
plan: Verify the issue by introducing a deliberate failure in `macos-test` and running `make macos-ci` locally
Refactor `macos-ci` in `Makefile` to use proper `make` dependencies: `macos-ci: macos-preflight ci macos-build macos-test` (note: macos-test needs to handle RALPH_UI_TESTS=0 correctly)
Ensure that `macos-test` and other sub-targets correctly return non-zero exit codes on failure
Verify that the final success message is only printed if all steps succeed
Perform a sweep of other targets in the `Makefile` to identify and fix similar failure-ignoring patterns
Run `make macos-ci` to verify the fix
tasks.request: Investigate and figure out why something like in the logs below is not causing the CI gate to be triggered and a response sent to the damn runner. The configured CI gate for this project is set in .ralph/config.json and is 'make macos-ci' So why the fuck does the loop complete as if nothing happened and nothing went wrong when it's very clear the fucking CI gate is failing. I don't think this is happening with other configured CI gate commands like 'make ci'

custom_fields.priority_reason: CI gate reporting false positives allows broken code to be merged

tasks.title: Fix all deprecated String(cString:) usages in Swift code
tasks.description: The macOS app build emits deprecation warnings for `String(cString:)` which is deprecated in favor of `String(decoding:array,as:)`. Currently these warnings are allowed to pass through CI without failing. The replacement API requires explicit handling of null-terminated C strings by truncating the null termination before decoding.
evidence: Warning: 'init(cString:)' is deprecated: Use String(decoding: array, as: UTF8.self) instead, after truncating the null termination
Found at CrashReporter.swift:195 in deviceModel() function
macos-ci passes despite deprecation warnings, violating CI contract
plan: Search for all occurrences of String(cString:) in apps/RalphMac directory
Replace with String(decoding: array, as: UTF8.self) pattern
Ensure proper null-termination truncation: dropLast() or prefix(upTo:) on the CChar array
Verify the replacement produces equivalent output to original code
Run unit tests for affected modules
Run make macos-ci to verify all deprecation warnings are resolved
Commit with message explaining API migration
notes: The deprecated API is less safe as it doesn't require explicit null-termination handling
New API forces safer C string handling by requiring array truncation before decoding
tasks.request: There are countless warnings like this that i see in the macos-ci. These are DISALLOWED. We should be seeing macos-ci fail when shit like this pops up. FIX THEM ALL AND MAKE SURE THE CI GATE FAILS WHEN THESE POP UP.

tasks.title: Enable Xcode warnings-as-errors in CI gate to prevent deprecation warnings
tasks.description: The macos-ci gate runs xcodebuild for both build and test phases, but currently allows warnings to pass without failing. This violates the repo's CI contract which states 'NO ERRORS OR WARNINGS ARE ACCEPTABLE' and 'warnings are errors' in AGENTS.md. Xcode warnings (including deprecation warnings) should cause the CI gate to fail, forcing fixes before commit.
evidence: AGENTS.md states: 'NO ERRORS OR WARNINGS ARE ACCEPTABLE'
AGENTS.md states: 'Clippy with -D warnings (warnings are errors)'
Makefile line 44: cargo clippy uses -D warnings flag (Rust side)
Makefile lines 152-164: xcodebuild for macos-build has no -warnings-as-errors flag
Makefile lines 166-189: xcodebuild for macos-test has no -warnings-as-errors flag
macos-ci passes despite emitting deprecation warnings like 'init(cString:)'
This creates a double-standard: Rust warnings fail CI, Swift warnings pass CI
plan: Add -warnings-as-errors=YES flag to xcodebuild in macos-build target
Add -warnings-as-errors=YES flag to xcodebuild in macos-test target
Verify the flag is correctly formatted for xcodebuild (Xcode 15+ syntax)
Run make macos-ci to confirm it now fails on the existing deprecation warning
Update AGENTS.md to document Xcode warnings-as-errors behavior if needed
Commit with message: 'Enable Xcode warnings-as-errors in CI gate'
notes: xcodebuild -warnings-as-errors=YES treats all compiler warnings as build failures
This aligns Swift/Xcode behavior with Rust Clippy -D warnings behavior
After this change, the CI gate will correctly fail on deprecation warnings until they are fixed
tasks.request: There are countless warnings like this that i see in the macos-ci. These are DISALLOWED. We should be seeing macos-ci fail when shit like this pops up. FIX THEM ALL AND MAKE SURE THE CI GATE FAILS WHEN THESE POP UP.

tasks.title: Fix session resume to handle dirty repo check before retry loop
tasks.description:
When resuming an incomplete session via `ralph run loop`, the system gets stuck in an infinite retry loop if the repo is dirty. The run loop detects the incomplete session, prompts the user to resume, but then immediately fails with 'repo is dirty' error. Instead of stopping gracefully or prompting to clean the repo, it retries the same task 50 times (the consecutive failure limit) with the same error each time before finally aborting.

The session resume logic should:
1. Check repo cleanliness BEFORE attempting to resume a session, OR
2. Treat 'repo is dirty' as a non-retryable error that immediately stops the loop with a clear message, OR
3. Re-prompt the user to clean the repo after the first failure

Currently, the loop wastes 50 retry attempts on an error that cannot self-resolve, providing poor UX and delaying the actual fix (cleaning the repo).
evidence: Log shows incomplete session detected for RQ-0446 (Phase 1, 0/1 iterations)
User chose to resume the session
Run loop immediately fails with 'repo is dirty; commit/stash your changes before running Ralph'
Error repeats 50 times with identical dirty repo status (same 5 tracked files + 1 untracked file)
Each retry selects RQ-0446 and resolves same phase settings (Phase 1/2/3 with Kimi/kimi-code)
Loop aborts only after hitting 50 consecutive failure limit
Log message: 'RunLoop: aborting after 50 consecutive failures'
Log message: 'Run loop aborted after 50 consecutive task failures. This usually indicates a systemic issue (e.g., repo dirty, runner misconfiguration, or interrupt flag stuck).'
User is frustrated because session resume offers no value if it just fails repeatedly without guidance
plan: Analyze run_loop.rs retry logic to understand how consecutive failures are handled
Identify where the 'repo is dirty' error is generated (likely in git.rs or early in run_one)
Determine best fix approach: pre-flight check vs non-retryable error classification
Implement fix: Add repo cleanliness check before session resume, OR classify dirty repo as fatal/non-retryable
Add user-friendly message when session resume is blocked by dirty repo
Write tests for session resume with dirty repo scenario
Verify fix by simulating: 1) Start task 2) Interrupt 3) Dirty repo 4) Resume attempt 5) Should fail gracefully with clear message, not 50 retries
notes: Consider adding 'Use --force to bypass' suggestion in the error message
May need to distinguish between recoverable errors (network, timeouts) vs non-recoverable (dirty repo, config errors)
tasks.request: What is the point of being able to resume a failed session if it doesnt work? See logs below: [logs showing 50 consecutive 'repo is dirty' failures when attempting to resume session for RQ-0446]

tasks.title: Add conflict detection for external task changes during editing
tasks.description: TaskDetailView does not detect when a task has been modified externally (via CLI or another app instance) while the user is editing it. This can lead to data loss when the user saves, overwriting the external changes. The app should warn users about conflicts and offer merge options.
evidence: TaskDetailView.swift lines 31-42: Draft task created from initial task, no update tracking
File watching triggers reload but doesn't check if user is currently editing
No mechanism to compare task version at save time vs. edit start time
plan: Store original task checksum/version when editing begins
On file change notification, compare current task with draft
Show conflict alert if task changed externally with options: overwrite, discard changes, or merge
Implement merge view showing diff between local and external changes
Add 'modified externally' indicator in UI when conflict detected
Write tests for conflict detection scenarios
Verify by: open task in app, modify via CLI, attempt save in app - conflict should appear

tasks.title: Add error recovery UI for failed CLI operations
tasks.description: When CLI operations fail (network issues, corrupted queue, permission errors), the app shows a simple error message but offers no recovery options. Users cannot retry, view detailed error logs, or get guidance on fixing the issue. This leaves users stuck when errors occur.
evidence: WorkspaceView.swift lines 284-290: Simple error display with no recovery actions
TaskListView.swift lines 225-232: Error shown but no retry mechanism
All error handling shows static text without actionable recovery steps
plan: Create reusable ErrorRecoveryView with retry, diagnose, and dismiss actions
Add detailed error logging with 'Copy Error Details' button
Implement retry with exponential backoff for transient failures
Add 'Diagnose' button that runs ralph queue validate and shows results
Show contextual help based on error type (permission, parse, network)
Add 'Open in Terminal' button to debug CLI issues manually
Write tests for error recovery flows
Verify by: induce CLI error (bad permissions), verify recovery UI appears with retry option

tasks.title: Add offline mode detection and graceful degradation
tasks.description: The macOS app does not detect when the bundled ralph CLI is unavailable or when the workspace directory is inaccessible. The app shows loading spinners indefinitely or crashes without clear feedback. The app should detect offline/unavailable state and show appropriate UI.
evidence: RalphCLIClient.swift lines 265-278: Validates executable but no ongoing availability checks
Workspace.swift loadTasks() hangs indefinitely if CLI fails to respond
No timeout handling for CLI operations in the UI layer
plan: Implement CLI health check on app launch and workspace switch
Add timeout (30s) for all CLI operations with user-cancelable progress
Create OfflineBannerView to show when CLI is unavailable
Add 'Retry Connection' button with visual feedback
Cache last known task list for offline viewing (read-only mode)
Show appropriate messages for different failure modes (no CLI, no workspace, permission denied)
Write tests for offline behavior simulation
Verify by: remove/move ralph binary, launch app - should show offline state with retry

tasks.title: Add menu bar extra for quick task access and status
tasks.description: Implement a menu bar extra (status bar item) that provides quick access to Ralph without opening the full app. Show current task status, next task title, and quick actions (run next, add quick task, open full app). This enables lightweight interaction for users who want to monitor and control Ralph from anywhere.
evidence: No menu bar integration exists - app must be open to see status
Menu bar extras are standard for background/utility apps on macOS
Users may want quick status without full app window
Menu bar provides always-available access
plan: Create MenuBarExtra using App protocol or NSStatusBar
Add Ralph icon to menu bar with status indicator
Show current/next task title in menu
Add 'Run Next Task' menu item with keyboard shortcut
Add 'Quick Add Task' menu item that opens input dialog
Show task counts by status (Todo: 5, Doing: 1, Done: 12)
Add 'Open Full App' menu item
Implement preferences toggle to enable/disable menu bar extra
Show notifications for task completion via menu bar
Add recent tasks submenu for quick access
Verify by clicking menu bar icon and testing all menu items

tasks.title: Implement global keyboard shortcuts and command palette
tasks.description: Add comprehensive keyboard shortcuts for power users including Cmd+N (new task), Cmd+R (run next), Cmd+K (command palette), Cmd+1/2/3 (switch views). Implement a command palette (Cmd+Shift+P or Cmd+K) that provides quick access to all app actions with fuzzy search. This enables efficient keyboard-driven workflows.
evidence: No keyboard shortcuts exist beyond system defaults
Power users expect keyboard-driven workflows
Command palettes are standard in modern dev tools (VS Code, Raycast)
Shortcuts significantly improve productivity for frequent actions
plan: Define keyboard shortcut scheme (Cmd+N new, Cmd+R run, Cmd+F find, Cmd+K palette)
Implement keyboard shortcut handlers in window
Create CommandPaletteView with fuzzy search
Register all app actions in palette with descriptions
Add shortcut indicators in menu items and tooltips
Implement view switching shortcuts (Cmd+1 Queue, Cmd+2 Run, Cmd+3 Analytics)
Add escape key handling to dismiss modals/palette
Implement space key to preview selected task
Add shortcuts for status changes (Cmd+Shift+S for status menu)
Document all shortcuts in Help menu
Verify by testing each shortcut and confirming action fires
